message.content:
ISTQB and k6/Grafana do **not** define one single universal, closed list of performance test types and checks for web applications. ISTQB defines the *performance-testing umbrella* and common test types such as **load, stress, spike, endurance/soak, scalability, capacity, and volume/concurrency**, while k6/Grafana documentation focuses more on **how to measure** performance with metrics such as **response time, percentiles, throughput, errors, virtual users, and long-running trends** rather than on a formal taxonomy of test types.[1][5]

| Test/check name | What it verifies | Subcategory | Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Performance testing** | Determines the performance efficiency of a component or system.[5] | Umbrella / overall performance testing | ISTQB Glossary |
| **Static performance testing** | Reviews performance requirements and artifacts before execution, including requirements, risks, database schema, ER diagrams, metadata, stored procedures, and queries.[4] | Static analysis / pre-execution | ISTQB tutorial on testing types in performance testing |
| **Dynamic performance testing** | Executes scenarios against the system to observe runtime behavior under load.[4] | Dynamic / execution-based | ISTQB tutorial on testing types in performance testing |
| **Load testing** | Verifies how the system handles increasing levels of anticipated, realistic load generated by a controlled number of concurrent users.[2] | Load | ISTQB tutorial on types of performance testing |
| **Stress testing** | Verifies behavior beyond the expected load limit to identify bottlenecks and the crash/failure point.[2] | Stress | ISTQB tutorial on types of performance testing |
| **Spike testing** | Verifies that the system responds correctly to sudden bursts of peak load and then returns to steady state.[2] | Spike | ISTQB tutorial on types of performance testing |
| **Endurance testing** | Verifies behavior over a long period with sustained activity, commonly used to reveal degradation over time.[2] | Soak / endurance | ISTQB tutorial on types of performance testing |
| **Scalability testing** | Verifies the system’s ability to meet future efficiency requirements as demand grows and capacity is expanded.[2] | Scalability | ISTQB tutorial on types of performance testing |
| **Concurrency testing** | Verifies that multiple users can access the system simultaneously; also used to refer to simultaneous data-volume activity.[2] | Concurrency / volume-related | ISTQB tutorial on types of performance testing |
| **Volume testing** | Verifies behavior when a large amount of data is added or processed at once.[2] | Volume | ISTQB tutorial on types of performance testing |
| **Capacity testing** | Verifies how many users or transactions a system can support while still meeting stated performance objectives, including data-volume objectives.[2] | Capacity | ISTQB tutorial on types of performance testing |
| **Performance requirements review** | Verifies that performance requirements are complete, accurate, and suitable for test design.[4] | Static / requirements validation | ISTQB tutorial on testing types in performance testing |
| **Risk review for performance** | Verifies performance risks are identified during planning and analysis.[4] | Static / risk analysis | ISTQB tutorial on testing types in performance testing |
| **Database schema and query review** | Verifies database structures, stored procedures, and queries for possible performance issues before execution.[4] | Static / design review | ISTQB tutorial on testing types in performance testing |
| **Response time** | Measures how long the system takes to respond to a request.[2] | Metric / runtime check | ISTQB tutorial on performance testing |
| **Resource utilization** | Measures CPU, memory, and related system-resource usage under load.[2] | Metric / runtime check | ISTQB tutorial on performance testing |
| **Throughput / transactions per second / hits per second** | Measures how much work the system completes in a unit of time.[2] | Metric / runtime check | ISTQB tutorial on performance testing |
| **Client-server interaction behavior** | Observes how the client and server behave under load, including delivery of pages/downloads and transaction flow.[2] | Runtime behavior | ISTQB tutorial on performance testing |
| **Percentile response times (p95/p99)** | Verifies tail latency, i.e. how slow the slowest 5% or 1% of requests are under load. | Metric / latency distribution | k6/Grafana documentation generally emphasizes percentiles and latency metrics; this is an inference from k6’s metric model and common load-testing practice, not a named ISTQB test type. |
| **Error rate under load** | Verifies how many requests fail while the system is under load. | Metric / reliability check | k6/Grafana documentation generally emphasizes error tracking and thresholds; this is an inference from k6’s metric model, not a named ISTQB test type. |
| **Concurrent virtual users** | Verifies behavior with a configured number of simultaneous simulated users. | Load / concurrency | k6/Grafana load-testing model; conceptually aligned with ISTQB concurrent-users load/concurrency testing. |
| **Ramp-up profile** | Verifies how the system behaves while load is gradually increased. | Load / ramping scenario | k6/Grafana scenario configuration; not a distinct ISTQB test type. |
| **Sustained-load memory growth** | Verifies whether memory usage grows over time during a long-running test, which can indicate a leak or degradation. | Endurance / soak | Inferred from endurance/soak testing goals; not explicitly named in ISTQB source excerpts provided. |
| **Autoscaling behavior** | Verifies whether the system scales out/in appropriately as load changes. | Scalability / capacity | Inferred from scalability/capacity testing goals; not explicitly named in the ISTQB source excerpts provided. |
| **Breakpoint / failure threshold** | Verifies the maximum load the system can tolerate before unacceptable failure or crash. | Stress / breakpoint | Inferred from stress testing definition as “beyond load limit” and finding the crash point.[2] |
| **Capacity limit** | Verifies the maximum sustainable workload while still meeting performance objectives. | Capacity | ISTQB capacity testing definition.[2] |
| **Baseline vs benchmark comparison** | Verifies current performance against an established baseline or benchmark to detect regressions or improvements. | Benchmarking / regression comparison | This is a standard performance-testing check, but it is **not explicitly named** in the cited ISTQB excerpts; it is a common interpretation consistent with performance-test practice. |

A few clarifications are important:

- ISTQB’s performance-testing material, as reflected in the provided sources, is **taxonomy-oriented**: it defines the major test types and the kinds of performance characteristics to observe.[1][2][5]
- k6/Grafana is **metric-and-scenario oriented**: it emphasizes measurements such as response times, throughput, error rates, and virtual-user patterns, including load ramps and long-duration tests, rather than issuing a formal list of “test types.”  
- **p95/p99, error rate, ramp-up, and concurrent virtual users** are best treated as **checks/metrics/scenario parameters**, not standalone ISTQB test types.[2]

If you want, I can turn this into a stricter matrix with three columns only—**ISTQB-defined test types**, **k6/Grafana checks/metrics**, and **overlap/notes**—so it is easier to use in a test plan or exam study sheet.
